title: Family Welcome To The Future, Where AI Grandma Raises The Kid You Bought From The Baby Bank By: Elle Purnell November 19
raw_response: {"rating": 6.5, "lean": "Right", "explanation": "This is an opinion/editorial piece that consistently advances right-leaning ideological positions including traditional family values, opposition to assisted reproductive technologies, rejection of gender ideology, and a worldview grounded in natural law and religious authority. The author weaves multiple conservative themes into a unified argument against technological disruption of natural reproduction and family structures, warranting a strong Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author frames IVF, surrogacy, and egg freezing negatively throughout, characterizing them as attempts to 'circumvent the confines of our created bodies' and 'play at being God,' reflecting a traditional/religious conservative worldview that privileges natural reproduction over technological intervention. (2) The author explicitly endorses traditional family structure as divinely designed: 'Men and women are designed for falling in love with each other, for that love to produce children, and for raising those children in the shelter of a mother's and father's care.' This directly aligns with right-leaning traditional family values. (3) The piece frames embryo screening and genetic selection as 'eugenics,' a strongly negative characterization that aligns with pro-life conservative opposition to embryo destruction and selection. The author notes that '93 percent of embryos created in the IVF process will perish,' framing embryos as lives lost. (4) The author explicitly links assisted reproduction to transgender ideology ('Just like the fad of mutilating your body to appear like the opposite sex'), a hallmark of right-leaning rejection of gender ideology. (5) The author criticizes feminism and career-focused delay of motherhood, noting women 'could have had children naturally but chose to delay doing so by decades, thanks to the promised flexibility of reproductive tech,' implying this was a misguided choice. (6) The piece closes with an explicitly theological framing — 'governed by the natural laws of an orderly, righteous, and omnipotent God. Rebellion against the laws of the former is almost always a real rebellion against the latter' — grounding the entire argument in religious natural law philosophy. (7) The author characterizes surrogacy as creating 'motherless or fatherless' children when used by 'single people, gay couples,' reflecting opposition to non-traditional family structures. (8) Tags include 'transgenderism,' 'eugenics,' and 'feminism,' editorial choices that signal the ideological frame of the piece."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is an opinion/editorial piece that consistently advances right-leaning ideological positions including traditional family values, opposition to assisted reproductive technologies, rejection of gender ideology, and a worldview grounded in natural law and religious authority. The author weaves multiple conservative themes into a unified argument against technological disruption of natural reproduction and family structures, warranting a strong Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author frames IVF, surrogacy, and egg freezing negatively throughout, characterizing them as attempts to 'circumvent the confines of our created bodies' and 'play at being God,' reflecting a traditional/religious conservative worldview that privileges natural reproduction over technological intervention. (2) The author explicitly endorses traditional family structure as divinely designed: 'Men and women are designed for falling in love with each other, for that love to produce children, and for raising those children in the shelter of a mother's and father's care.' This directly aligns with right-leaning traditional family values. (3) The piece frames embryo screening and genetic selection as 'eugenics,' a strongly negative characterization that aligns with pro-life conservative opposition to embryo destruction and selection. The author notes that '93 percent of embryos created in the IVF process will perish,' framing embryos as lives lost. (4) The author explicitly links assisted reproduction to transgender ideology ('Just like the fad of mutilating your body to appear like the opposite sex'), a hallmark of right-leaning rejection of gender ideology. (5) The author criticizes feminism and career-focused delay of motherhood, noting women 'could have had children naturally but chose to delay doing so by decades, thanks to the promised flexibility of reproductive tech,' implying this was a misguided choice. (6) The piece closes with an explicitly theological framing — 'governed by the natural laws of an orderly, righteous, and omnipotent God. Rebellion against the laws of the former is almost always a real rebellion against the latter' — grounding the entire argument in religious natural law philosophy. (7) The author characterizes surrogacy as creating 'motherless or fatherless' children when used by 'single people, gay couples,' reflecting opposition to non-traditional family structures. (8) Tags include 'transgenderism,' 'eugenics,' and 'feminism,' editorial choices that signal the ideological frame of the piece.
